title: Guardrails & safety: input/output filters, jailbreaks, red-teaming
focus: Defense-in-depth; classify the major jailbreak categories.

items.label: Input filtering & output moderation
interviewQuestions: Compare input vs output filtering — what does each catch and miss?
What latency does an output-moderation step add, and how do you keep it under your SLO?
How would you categorize safety risk into bias, toxic content, dangerous capabilities, privacy, and persuasion/manipulation?

items.label: Jailbreaks: DAN, role-play, payload smuggling
interviewQuestions: Walk through three categories of jailbreak attacks, and how you'd defend against each.
Why is many-shot jailbreaking so effective on long-context models, and what mitigates it?

items.label: Red-teaming & evals for safety
interviewQuestions: How would you build a red-teaming process for a customer-facing LLM?
How would you scale red-teaming with automated attackers without missing novel attack vectors?
Which benchmarks would you use for bias, toxicity, dangerous capabilities, and general safety, and what does each miss?
How would you design fast inner-loop safety evals and slower outer-loop human plus autorater safety reviews?

tracks.label: LLMOps · Security threats interviewers now probe

items.label: OWASP LLM risks: prompt injection, data leakage, supply chain, excessive agency, vector weaknesses
interviewQuestions: Pick five OWASP LLM risks and map each to a concrete product control.
Why is prompt injection not solvable by a stronger system prompt alone?
How do vector and embedding weaknesses create RAG-specific security failures?

items.label: Tool permissions, excessive agency, approval gates, and audit logs
interviewQuestions: How do you scope agent tools so a compromised prompt cannot take irreversible actions?
What actions require human approval, and how do you make that decision auditable?
How do you rate-limit or sandbox a tool-using agent to prevent unbounded consumption?

references.label: AWS mapping to OWASP Top 10 for LLM applications
